Traumatic Brain Hemorrhages

Interactive Neurosurgery Reference — PGY-1 Level

Meningeal Layers — Cross-Section | Image: Radiopaedia.org, CC-BY-NC-SA
Meningeal Layers — Coronal View | Image: Radiopaedia.org, CC-BY-NC-SA
EDH Acute SDH Subacute SDH Chronic SDH tSAH Contusion DAI
1

Epidural Hematoma (EDH)

Blood between inner skull table and periosteal dura, most commonly from middle meningeal artery laceration at the pterion.
Biconvex / Lens-shaped Does NOT cross sutures Lucid interval 20-50% Skull fracture 85-95% Mortality: 5-12% (treated)
Image: Radiopaedia.org, CC-BY-NC-SA

Pathophysiology

The epidural hematoma represents a collection of blood between the inner table of the skull and the periosteal (outer) layer of the dura mater. The middle meningeal artery (MMA) is the most commonly implicated vessel, entering the skull through the foramen spinosum and coursing along the inner table of the temporal bone within a groove (or sometimes a bony canal). The MMA divides into anterior and posterior branches approximately 1-2 cm above the zygomatic arch. The anterior branch is most frequently injured because it courses directly beneath the pterion — the thinnest point of the skull (2-3 mm thick), formed by the junction of the frontal, parietal, temporal, and greater wing of the sphenoid bones.

The arterial nature of most EDHs explains their rapid, life-threatening expansion. The MMA operates at systemic arterial pressure (mean ~80-100 mmHg), which is far greater than the adhesive force between the periosteal dura and the inner skull table. In adults, dural adhesion is relatively loose in the temporal and parietal regions but becomes progressively more adherent at suture lines. As arterial blood pumps into the epidural space under high pressure, it progressively strips the dura away from the inner table, creating an expanding lens-shaped mass. An arterial EDH can expand from negligible to life-threatening (>30 mL) within 30-60 minutes. The critical volume for neurological deterioration is approximately 25-30 mL in adults, though temporal location can cause herniation at smaller volumes.

EDHs do NOT cross suture lines because the periosteal dura is tightly fused to the skull at sutures. However, they CAN cross the midline in frontal/occipital regions and can cross the skull base.

Venous Epidural Hematomas (10-15%)

Most commonly in the posterior fossa from transverse/sigmoid sinus tears. Expand more slowly (venous pressure 5-15 mmHg) but exceptionally dangerous:

  • Posterior fossa volume only ~150 mL total
  • Can obstruct CSF outflow at 4th ventricle → acute obstructive hydrocephalus
  • Can cause tonsillar herniation through foramen magnum with minimal warning

Epidemiology

  • Peak incidence: 20-30 years. Rare <2 years (dura tightly adherent) and >60 (dura increasingly adherent).
  • Skull fracture: 85-95% of adult cases; up to 40% without fracture in children <5.
  • Location: Temporal 70-80%, frontal 10%, posterior fossa 5-10%.
  • Incidence: 1-4% of all TBI, 5-15% of fatal TBI. Male 4:1.

The "Lucid Interval" (20-50% of cases)

The classic triphasic presentation:

  1. Phase 1 — Initial concussion (minutes): Brief LOC from concussive force to the reticular activating system, NOT from the hematoma.
  2. Phase 2 — Lucid interval (minutes to hours, up to 12h): Patient regains consciousness. May appear normal or have mild headache. Hematoma is actively expanding but hasn't reached critical volume. Critical window for diagnosis and intervention.
  3. Phase 3 — Rapid deterioration (minutes): Brain compliance exhausted → exponential ICP rise → uncal herniation. Without intervention: death in 1-2 hours.
StageClinical Features
Early (expanding hematoma, ICP rising)Progressive headache, nausea/vomiting (area postrema), increasing drowsiness, irritability
Middle (uncal herniation beginning)Ipsilateral pupil dilation (CN III parasympathetic compression first), contralateral hemiparesis, decreasing GCS, ipsilateral ptosis
Late (brainstem compression)Bilateral fixed dilated pupils, decerebrate posturing, Cushing triad, respiratory arrest
~30% never lose consciousness initially. ~20% are unconscious from the start (severe associated injury). The lucid interval is NOT pathognomonic for EDH. Kernohan notch phenomenon (~5%): contralateral peduncle compressed against tentorial edge → IPSILATERAL hemiparesis (false localizing sign).

Posterior Fossa EDH

  • Severe occipital headache, neck stiffness
  • Cerebellar signs: ataxia, nystagmus, dysmetria
  • CN VI, VII palsies
  • Rapid deterioration without warning — tonsillar herniation or acute obstructive hydrocephalus
  • Upbeat nystagmus, hiccups, respiratory irregularity = ominous brainstem signs

CT Findings

EDH CT — Radiopaedia.org, CC-BY-NC-SA

Classic: Biconvex (lens-shaped) hyperdense collection

Confined between inner table and stripped dura. Dural attachments at suture lines prevent lateral spread, forcing central expansion.

Density Characteristics

  • Hyperacute (min): May be isodense (not yet clotted)
  • Acute (hrs-3d): Hyperdense, 60-80 HU. Bright white.
  • "Swirl sign": Mixed hyper/hypodense = active bleeding = surgical emergency (90% sensitivity)
  • Subacute (3-14d): Isodense (30-40 HU)
  • Chronic (>14d): Hypodense (10-20 HU)

Key CT Features

  • Does NOT cross suture lines (vs. SDH)
  • CAN cross midline (frontal/occipital) and tentorium (rare)
  • Look for: temporal bone fracture on bone windows, mass effect, midline shift, uncal herniation signs
  • Measure: Max thickness (perpendicular to inner table), midline shift (septum pellucidum)

Associated Findings

  • Linear temporal bone fracture (85-95%)
  • Underlying brain contusion (20-30%)
  • Small acute SDH on same side possible
  • Pneumocephalus if fracture communicates with sinuses

Surgical Management (BTF Guidelines)

Indications for Emergent Evacuation

  • Thickness >15 mm
  • Midline shift >5 mm
  • GCS <9 with anisocoria
  • Temporal location with neurological deterioration (even if <15 mm)
  • Any EDH with acute neurological decline
  • Posterior fossa EDH with any mass effect or symptoms (LOW threshold)

Surgical Technique

Craniotomy is standard (not burr holes). Large bone flap for: (1) complete evacuation, (2) MMA cauterization/clipping, (3) brain inspection, (4) tack-up sutures. Burr holes only as temporizing emergency measure. Bone flap is replaced (no need for DC in pure EDH).

Conservative Management (ALL criteria must be met)

CriterionThreshold
Thickness<15 mm
Midline shift<5 mm
GCS>8 (some centers >12)
Focal deficitNone
AnisocoriaNone
LocationNot temporal (or very small temporal, observed carefully)

Conservative Protocol

  • ICU admission (min 48-72h)
  • Neuro checks q1h (GCS, pupils, motor)
  • Serial CT: 6-8h, 24h, 48h minimum
  • HOB 30°, SBP target 100-140
  • Seizure prophylaxis: Levetiracetam 500 mg IV/PO BID x 7 days
  • Pain: acetaminophen first-line, avoid NSAIDs
  • Correct any coagulopathy
Golden Hour: Mortality approximately doubles per hour of delay. Pupil dilation to surgery <70 min = good outcome; >70 min = poor. Pre-coma surgery: 5-10% mortality. Post-coma: 20-40%. Bilateral fixed pupils: >75% mortality.

Postoperative Care

  • ICP monitoring if GCS ≤8 preop or associated brain injury
  • CT within 24h postop (sooner if change)
  • Seizure prophylaxis x 7 days
  • DVT: SCDs immediately, LMWH at 24-48h if CT stable

Prognosis

  • Mortality: 5-12% with surgery; near 100% untreated herniation
  • Functional outcome: BEST of all traumatic ICH when caught early. GOS 4-5 in >85-90% operated BEFORE herniation.
FactorGoodPoor
GCS at surgeryGCS 15: ~97% goodGCS 3: ~25% good
Pupil reactivityBilateral reactiveBilateral fixed (not hopeless if operated in minutes)
Time to surgery<2 hours>2 hours
AgeChildren betterElderly worse
Associated injuryPure EDHEDH + SDH/contusion

Recurrence: ~1-3% with craniotomy + tack-up sutures; higher with burr holes only.

EDH has the best prognosis of all traumatic intracranial hemorrhages when caught early because the underlying brain is often minimally injured. Outcome depends almost entirely on speed of diagnosis and surgical intervention.
2

Acute Subdural Hematoma (aSDH)

Blood in subdural space from torn bridging veins or cortical arteries. The deadliest traumatic intracranial hemorrhage.
Crescent-shaped CROSSES suture lines Usually NO lucid interval 4-hour rule Mortality: 50-90%
Image: Radiopaedia.org, CC-BY-NC-SA

Pathophysiology

Most commonly from tearing of bridging veins (superior cerebral veins) that traverse the subdural space for 1-3 cm before emptying into the superior sagittal sinus. During rapid acceleration-deceleration, the brain moves relative to the skull, creating shear forces that tear these veins at their fixed-to-mobile junction.

Brain atrophy (elderly, alcoholics) significantly increases vulnerability — bridging veins must traverse a longer distance, are under greater baseline tension, and tear with even trivial trauma. The bridging veins also have thinner walls and reduced elasticity with age.

~20-30% have an arterial component (torn cortical arteries/MCA branches). These expand more rapidly, are thicker, and carry significantly worse prognosis due to rapid expansion and associated cortical injury.

Key anatomical distinction from EDH: Subdural space is continuous — no suture-line barriers. SDH crosses suture lines freely but does NOT cross midline (falx cerebri barrier). Does not cross tentorium.

Epidemiology

  • Bimodal: Young adults (MVA, assault) and elderly >65 (falls >70%)
  • Anticoagulation: 7-10x risk increase (warfarin most common). DOACs also increase risk.
  • Antiplatelets: 2-4x risk increase. DAPT more than either alone.
  • 3-5x more common than EDH. Associated brain injury in 50-80%. Male 3:1.
  • Mortality: 50-90% — deadliest traumatic intracranial hemorrhage. Best series: 30-40%.

Presentation

Usually NO lucid interval (70-80%). Forces required to tear bridging veins almost always cause significant primary brain injury (contusions, DAI), so impairment is immediate.

  • GCS: Usually low (median 5-8). >50% present GCS ≤8.
  • Focal deficits: Contralateral hemiparesis, aphasia (dominant hemisphere), homonymous hemianopia
  • Herniation at admission (30-50%): Ipsilateral pupil dilation (CN III), contralateral hemiparesis → decerebrate, Cushing response, bilateral fixed dilated pupils
  • Seizures: 10-15% at presentation; 20-30% within first week
A thin aSDH with disproportionate midline shift strongly suggests severe underlying brain edema (worse prognosis than thick aSDH with proportionate shift). "Hyperacute" SDH with mixed density = active arterial bleeding — extremely time-sensitive. Bilateral in 15-20% (especially elderly, anticoagulated).

CT Findings

Acute SDH — Radiopaedia.org, CC-BY-NC-SA

Classic: Crescent-shaped (concavo-convex) hyperdense collection

Blood spreads freely along subdural space, conforming to concave inner skull and convex brain surface.

Density Evolution

  • Acute (0-3d): Hyperdense, 50-70 HU
  • Subacute (3-21d): Isodense (7-14d) — DANGER ZONE, nearly invisible on CT
  • Chronic (>21d): Hypodense, 10-25 HU

Key Features

  • CROSSES suture lines (key distinction from EDH)
  • Does NOT cross midline (falx)
  • Can extend along falx (interhemispheric) or tentorium
Midline shift vs. SDH thickness: Shift EXCEEDS thickness = severe brain edema (poor prognosis). Shift LESS than thickness = relatively better. Example: 8mm SDH with 15mm shift = brain edema contributing significantly.

Mixed Density Patterns

  • Swirl sign = active bleeding
  • Acute-on-chronic = fresh hyperdense blood in hypodense chronic collection
  • Heterogeneous = coagulopathy, repeated bleeding, different ages

Associated: Contusions (50-80%), cerebral edema, tSAH, DAI

Surgical Criteria (BTF / Bullock)

  • SDH thickness >10 mm
  • Midline shift >5 mm
  • GCS drop ≥2 from injury to hospital
  • ICP >20 mmHg despite medical management
  • Asymmetric or fixed dilated pupil attributable to SDH

Surgical Technique

Large trauma craniotomy (frontotemporoparietal "question mark" flap) — NOT burr holes. Complete clot evacuation, identify/coagulate bleeding source, inspect brain, assess swelling. If brain severely swollen after evacuation: decompressive craniectomy (bone flap left off, duraplasty). Bone stored for cranioplasty at 6-12 weeks.

Anticoagulation Reversal — IMMEDIATE

AgentReversalDoseNotes
Warfarin4-factor PCC + Vit KPCC: 25 u/kg (INR 2-4), 35 u/kg (INR 4-6), 50 u/kg (INR >6). Vit K: 10 mg IV over 20 min.Do NOT use FFP as primary. PCC reverses in 15-30 min. Vit K prevents INR rebound (6-12h onset).
DabigatranIdarucizumab (Praxbind)5 g IV (two 2.5g boluses, ≤15 min apart)100% reversal in minutes. If unavailable: aPCC (FEIBA) 50 u/kg or hemodialysis (35% dialyzable).
Rivaroxaban / ApixabanAndexanet alfa or 4F-PCCAndexanet: 400-800 mg bolus + infusion. PCC: 50 u/kg.Many centers use PCC first-line (cheaper, faster). Andexanet costs $25-50K.
Heparin (UFH)Protamine sulfate1 mg per 100 units heparin (last 2-3h). Max 50 mg.Slow IV push over 10 min. Anaphylaxis risk (fish allergy, prior exposure).
EnoxaparinProtamine sulfate1 mg per 1 mg enoxaparin (last 8h)Only 60-75% reversal. If >8h since dose: 0.5 mg per 1 mg.
AntiplateletsPlatelets + DDAVP1 apheresis unit. DDAVP 0.3 mcg/kg IV.PATCH trial: worse outcomes in spontaneous ICH. Used for surgical hemostasis.
ThrombocytopeniaPlatelet transfusionTarget >100K for neurosurgeryTransfuse aggressively before/during surgery.
The 4-Hour Rule: Every 30 min delay increases mortality. Surgery within 4h: ~30% mortality. After 4h: ~90%. Some studies: door-to-OR <90-120 min is optimal.

Postoperative

  • ICP monitoring (EVD or bolt) if GCS ≤8 preop or associated injury
  • CT within 24h postop. Seizure prophylaxis x 7d.
  • DVT: SCDs immediately, LMWH at 24-48h if stable

Prognosis

FactorGood PrognosisPoor Prognosis
GCS at surgery (#1)GCS >8: ~30% mortalityGCS 3-5: ~90% mortality
Age<40: ~20% mortality>65: ~75% mortality
Time to surgery<4h: ~30%>4h: ~90%
PupilsBilateral reactiveBilateral fixed: ~90%
Midline shift<10mm>10mm: poor regardless
Associated injuryIsolated SDHSDH + contusion + DAI
AnticoagulationNone2-3x higher mortality
Basal cisternsEffaced but visibleAbsent = brainstem compression

Functional outcome: GOS 4-5 in only 40-60% of survivors. Many have significant cognitive deficits, personality changes. Rehabilitation takes months to years.

3

Subacute Subdural Hematoma

Transitional phase (3-21 days). The isodense danger zone at days 7-14 makes this nearly invisible on CT.
Days 3-21 Isodense (7-14d) MRI superior Insidious onset

Pathophysiology & Timing (3-21 days)

DaysHemoglobin ProductCT Density
1-3Oxyhemoglobin (intact RBCs)Hyperdense (60 HU)
3-7DeoxyhemoglobinDropping: 60 → 40 HU
7-14Methemoglobin (extracellular)ISODENSE (30-40 HU) — DANGER ZONE
14-21Continued degradationHypodense

Fibroblasts invade clot margins, forming a neomembrane with fragile neovascular channels (transition to chronic SDH pathophysiology).

Presentation

More insidious than acute SDH:

  • Progressive headache over days
  • Gradually declining cognition, confusion, personality changes
  • New focal deficit (subtle hemiparesis, speech difficulty)
  • Gait instability, drowsiness → obtundation
  • Seizures (10-15%)
Often misdiagnosed as stroke, dementia, depression, or medication effect, especially in the elderly.

The Isodense Danger

A 15-20 mm SDH can be essentially invisible on CT during the isodense phase (days 7-14).

Clues to Isodense SDH on CT

  • Effaced cortical sulci on affected side (asymmetric)
  • Medial displacement of gray-white junction from inner table
  • Compressed ipsilateral ventricle + contralateral dilation
  • Subtle midline shift without obvious cause
  • "White matter buckling sign"
  • Inward displacement of cortical veins (contrast CT)

MRI is Far Superior

  • T1: Hyperintense (bright) from methemoglobin — clearly visible
  • FLAIR: Hyperintense
  • If clinical suspicion + equivocal CT = get MRI

Management

  • Symptomatic / mass effect: Surgical evacuation
    • Craniotomy for organized/solid clots (earlier subacute)
    • Burr hole drainage for liquefied collections (later subacute)
  • Asymptomatic, small: Serial imaging q3-7 days, close follow-up
  • Hold anticoagulation until stable. Restart 2-4 weeks.
  • Postop: subdural drain 24-48h, CT before drain removal

Pearls

The isodense phase is the most diagnostically treacherous period. Always look for indirect signs (effaced sulci, displaced gray-white junction, asymmetric ventricles) and get MRI if suspicious.
4

Chronic Subdural Hematoma (cSDH)

Self-perpetuating biological process >21 days: neomembrane, neovascularization, recurrent micro-hemorrhages. The "great neurological imitator."
Hypodense crescent Neomembrane cycle ~30% recurrence MMA embolization Mortality: 0-5% (treated)
Image: Radiopaedia.org, CC-BY-NC-SA

The Neomembrane Cycle

  1. Dural border cell activation (d3-7): Cells lack tight junctions, begin proliferating
  2. Outer membrane formation (d7-14): Thick, richly vascular (VEGF-driven neovascularization)
  3. Inner membrane formation (d14-21): Thinner, less vascular
  4. Fragile neovessels: Immature, lack basement membranes → continuously leak → self-perpetuating cycle: fragile vessels bleed → inflammation → more VEGF → more neovascularization → more bleeding
  5. Osmotic expansion: Degrading blood products create high osmotic gradient + increased fibrinolytic activity (tPA) keeps collection liquefied

Why cSDH Recurs (~30%)

Burr holes remove liquid but leave membranes. Outer membrane continues bleeding. Brain may not re-expand (atrophy). Risk factors: bilateral, anticoagulation, atrophy, age >80, diabetes.

Epidemiology

  • Peak: 70-80 years. Up to 58/100K/year in >70.
  • Trauma history in only 50-70% (often trivial, forgotten).
  • Bilateral in 15-25%.
  • Risk factors: age >60, atrophy, alcoholism, anticoagulation, hemodialysis, CSF shunts, prior craniotomy.

The "Great Neurological Imitator"

  • Cognitive decline / dementia-like (most common): Confusion, memory loss, personality change
  • Focal deficit: Hemiparesis, aphasia — mimics stroke
  • Gait disturbance: Mimics NPH or Parkinson's
  • Headache: Progressive, worse mornings, worse with Valsalva
  • Seizures: New-onset in elderly (10-15%)
  • Fluctuating mental status: "Good days and bad days" — hallmark of cSDH
  • Falls: Both cause and consequence
  • Incontinence: Bilateral cSDH mimics NPH triad
Any elderly patient with progressive cognitive decline, new neuro deficit, or personality change should have a CT head to rule out cSDH. One of the most treatable causes of "dementia."

CT Findings

Chronic SDH — Radiopaedia.org, CC-BY-NC-SA
  • Hypodense (10-25 HU), similar to CSF density
  • Crescent-shaped, follows convexity
  • Crosses suture lines, NOT midline
  • Mixed density:
    • Homogeneously hypodense = simple chronic
    • Layering (dense dependent, light superior) = acute-on-chronic
    • Septations = multiple re-bleeds (burr holes less effective)
    • "Trabeculated" = organized, septated
  • Bilateral: Midline shift may be deceptively minimal

Surgical: Burr Hole Drainage (First-Line)

  1. 1-2 burr holes over collection (frontal + parietal)
  2. Dura opened cruciate, outer membrane incised
  3. Gentle irrigation with warm saline until clear
  4. Subdural drain placement — reduces recurrence from ~30% to ~10% (multiple RCTs)
  5. Drain removed at 24-48h. Repeat CT before removal.

Craniotomy reserved for: Organized/solid clots, heavily septated, recurrent after burr holes, calcified ("armored brain").

MMA Embolization (Game-Changer)

Eliminates blood supply to neomembrane fragile vessels via interventional neuroradiology.

TrialYearResult
EMBOLISE2024 (Lancet)Recurrence needing re-op: 4.3% (embolization + surgery) vs. 11.3% (surgery alone)
STEM2024 (NEJM)~50% reduction in recurrence/treatment failure

Indications: Adjunct to burr holes, primary for small-moderate cSDH, recurrent cSDH, bilateral cSDH, patients on anticoagulation.

Dexamethasone: NOT Recommended

Dex-CSDH (2020, NEJM): Dexamethasone had WORSE outcomes at 6 months + more adverse events. Not routine.

Bilateral cSDH

Most surgeons drain both sides simultaneously. Monitor for re-accumulation on opposite side after unilateral drainage.

Prognosis

  • Mortality: 0-5% with treatment
  • Functional recovery: 80-90% return to baseline
  • Recurrence: ~30% without drain, ~10% with drain, ~5% with drain + MMA embolization
  • Recurrence typically within 1-3 months
  • Calcified ("armored") cSDH: rare, very difficult to treat
5

Traumatic Subarachnoid Hemorrhage (tSAH)

Blood in subarachnoid space from torn pial/cortical vessels. Convexity distribution (vs. aneurysmal SAH in basal cisterns). Monitor for vasospasm and hydrocephalus.
Convexity sulci Vasospasm 2-10% Hydrocephalus 8-25% Depends on severity
Image: Radiopaedia.org, CC-BY-NC-SA

Pathophysiology

tSAH results from disruption of small pial vessels and cortical arteries during impact. Blood collects between arachnoid and pia, mixing with CSF. Unlike aneurysmal SAH (single large arterial source into basal cisterns), tSAH is from multiple small-vessel tears over cortical convexities.

Traumatic vs. Aneurysmal SAH — Critical Distinction

FeatureTraumatic SAHAneurysmal SAH
DistributionConvexity sulci, peripheralBasal cisterns, Sylvian fissure, symmetric
ContextClear trauma"Worst headache of my life"
AmountUsually thin, in sulciOften thick, filling cisterns
PatternLinear, convexityStar-shaped, basal cisterns
AssociatedContusions, SDH, fractureClean brain otherwise
When to get CTA even with trauma: Basal cistern > convexity blood, disproportionate SAH to mechanism, thick Sylvian fissure blood (MCA aneurysm), young + minimal trauma (aneurysm CAUSED the fall?), interhemispheric SAH (AComm), thunderclap headache preceding trauma.

Vasospasm in tSAH

  • Angiographic: 18-50%; Symptomatic: 2-10% (much lower than aneurysmal)
  • Peaks at days 3-7 (earlier than aneurysmal 7-10)
  • Monitor: TCD daily — MCA velocity >120 cm/s = vasospasm; >200 = severe. Lindegaard ratio >3 confirms.
  • Nimodipine 60 mg PO/NG q4h x 21d (weaker evidence than aneurysmal but commonly used)

Post-Traumatic Hydrocephalus (8-25%)

  • Mechanism: Blood products clog arachnoid granulations → communicating hydrocephalus
  • Acute: IVH blocking 4th ventricle/aqueduct (obstructive)
  • Chronic: Weeks-months, communicating. NPH triad if chronic.
  • Diagnosis: Ventriculomegaly, Evans index >0.3, transependymal CSF flow
  • Treatment: EVD (acute), VP shunt (chronic). 10-15% need permanent diversion.

CT Findings

tSAH CT — Radiopaedia.org, CC-BY-NC-SA

Linear hyperdensity in cortical sulci, predominantly convexities. Asymmetric, adjacent to impact site.

Modified Fisher Scale

GradeDescriptionVasospasm Risk
0No SAH or IVHLow
1Thin SAH, no IVH~15%
2Thin SAH + IVH~25%
3Thick SAH, no IVH~30%
4Thick SAH + IVH~40%

Management

  • ABCs, stabilize, treat associated injuries
  • Repeat CT at 6-12h (tSAH can expand, contusions blossom)
  • Seizure prophylaxis: Levetiracetam 500 mg BID x 7d (preferred over phenytoin)
  • Monitor for vasospasm (TCDs) if significant SAH
  • Monitor for hydrocephalus
  • CTA if aneurysmal distribution
  • ICP monitoring if GCS ≤8
  • BP: avoid SBP >160 (worsens bleeding) and <90 (secondary injury)

Seizure Prophylaxis Details

  • 7 days only — no benefit extending beyond for early seizure prevention
  • Early seizures (within 7d): 10-15%. Late/post-traumatic epilepsy: 5-10%.
  • Risk factors for late seizures: cortical contusion, depressed fracture, prolonged coma, SDH, seizure within first week

Pearls

Always differentiate from aneurysmal SAH. Basal cistern blood with trauma = get CTA. A young patient with SAH and "minor fall" may have had an aneurysm rupture that CAUSED the fall.
Vasospasm peaks earlier (days 3-7) than aneurysmal (7-10). Symptomatic vasospasm is much less common (2-10% vs. 20-30%).
6

Intraparenchymal Hemorrhage / Cerebral Contusion

Coup and contrecoup hemorrhagic cortical injury. Contusions "blossom" in 40-60% — can double/triple over 24-72h.
Coup & contrecoup Temporal/frontal poles Blossom 40-60% Mortality: 25-50% (surgical)
Image: Radiopaedia.org, CC-BY-NC-SA

Coup and Contrecoup

Coup: At impact site. Skull deforms inward, compressing underlying brain.

Contrecoup: Opposite side, often MORE severe. Negative pressure cavitation + direct impact against opposite inner table. Most common in anterior temporal poles and orbitofrontal cortex (rough skull base with bony ridges of sphenoid wing and orbital plates).

Classic pattern: Occipital blow → frontal and temporal pole contrecoup contusions.

Locations by Frequency

  1. Anterior temporal poles (45%)
  2. Orbitofrontal cortex (30%)
  3. Frontal convexity (15%)
  4. Parasagittal cortex (5%)
  5. Cerebellar/Occipital (rare, <5%)

Contusion Blossoming (40-60%)

Hemorrhagic progression of contusion (HPC) — major cause of delayed deterioration.

Mechanisms

  • Primary microvascular injury with delayed bleeding (vasospasm resolves, BP normalizes)
  • BBB breakdown → vasogenic edema → compresses microvessels → ischemia → more BBB damage (vicious cycle)
  • Local coagulation cascade exhaustion
  • Inflammatory mediators (cytokines, MMPs) degrade BBB

Timeline

  • 6-12h: ~50% of blossoming visible
  • 12-24h: ~80% has occurred
  • 24-72h: Remaining 20%
  • >72h: Uncommon — consider coagulopathy or alternate diagnosis

Contusions can double or triple: 10 mL → 25-30 mL at 24h. Temporal contusions especially dangerous (uncal herniation).

Delayed Traumatic ICH (DTICH)

New hemorrhagic lesion on follow-up CT not present initially. Incidence 5-10%. Typically 24-72h, can occur up to 2 weeks. This is why repeat CT at 6-8h is mandatory even if initial CT is normal.

Risk factors: elderly, anticoagulation, coagulopathy, hypotension, decompressive surgery.

Risk Factors for Blossoming

  • Initial size >10 mL
  • Coagulopathy (INR >1.2, platelets <100K, anticoagulants)
  • Multiple contusions
  • tSAH on initial CT
  • "Salt and pepper" pattern (mixed density = ongoing micro-hemorrhage)

CT Findings

Contusion — Radiopaedia.org, CC-BY-NC-SA

Irregular, heterogeneous (mixed hyper/hypodense) cortical lesion. "Salt and pepper" pattern suggests ongoing micro-hemorrhage. Typically in frontal and temporal poles.

Look for associated: tSAH in adjacent sulci, SDH, edema (loss of gray-white differentiation).

Surgical Criteria (BTF)

  • Temporal contusion >20 mL with mass effect (lowest threshold — uncal herniation risk)
  • Any location >50 mL with significant mass effect
  • Progressive neuro decline from contusion
  • Refractory ICP >20 despite maximal medical Rx
  • GCS ≤8 with midline shift >5 mm
  • Effaced basal cisterns

Surgical Technique

Craniotomy + gentle contusion evacuation (only necrotic tissue). Partial temporal lobectomy for temporal contusions (non-dominant tip = low morbidity). DC if severe swelling.

Non-Operative Management

  • ICP monitoring: mandatory GCS ≤8 + abnormal CT
  • Serial CT: 6h, 24h, 48-72h minimum
  • Osmotherapy: Mannitol (0.25-1 g/kg q4-6h, hold osm >320) or HTS (3% targeting Na 145-155)
  • Avoid secondary insults: SBP <90, PaO2 <60, temp >38C, glucose >180, Hgb <7-10
  • Seizure prophylaxis, correct coagulopathy

Decompressive Craniectomy Trials

TrialResultTake-Home
DECRA (2011, NEJM)Early DC for diffuse TBI with ICP >20: reduced ICP but WORSE outcomesDo NOT use DC early/prophylactically for diffuse injury
RESCUEicp (2016, NEJM)DC for refractory ICP >25 after maximal Rx: mortality 27% vs 49%, but more vegetative/severe disabilityDC is life-saving last resort. Saves lives but with disability spectrum.

BTF 2020: DC is last-tier for refractory ICP >25 after all medical therapies have failed.

Pearls

Temporal contusions have the lowest volume threshold for surgery (>20 mL vs. >50 mL elsewhere) because of proximity to the uncus and risk of uncal herniation.
DECRA = early DC bad for diffuse. RESCUEicp = late DC saves lives with disability. Key distinction: DECRA was early + diffuse; RESCUEicp was last-resort + refractory.
7

Diffuse Axonal Injury (DAI)

Rotational acceleration-deceleration shearing axons at gray-white junction, corpus callosum, brainstem. CT often NORMAL — MRI SWI is gold standard.
CT often normal MRI SWI gold standard 3 Adams grades Prevent secondary insults Mortality: 15-55% by grade
DAI CT — Radiopaedia.org, CC-BY-NC-SA
DAI MRI (SWI) — Radiopaedia.org, CC-BY-NC-SA

Pathophysiology

Primary axotomy (immediate): Mechanical disruption at gray-white interfaces.

Secondary axotomy (delayed, hours-weeks — dominant, potentially modifiable):

  1. Axolemmal disruption: Stretching opens mechanosensitive Ca/Na channels → massive Ca2+ influx
  2. Calcium-mediated injury: Calpains degrade cytoskeleton (neurofilaments, microtubules) → disrupted transport
  3. Axonal swelling: Organelles accumulate → "axonal retraction balls" (beta-APP staining)
  4. Secondary disconnection: Swollen axon disconnects (hours-days)
  5. Wallerian degeneration: Distal segment degenerates (days-weeks)

Adams Grading System

GradeLocationForceMortality
1Gray-white junction (frontal, temporal)Mildest rotational15%
2+ Corpus callosum (body, splenium)Moderate (falx concentrates shear)25%
3+ Brainstem (dorsolateral midbrain, upper pons)Severe (tentorium concentrates shear)55%

Biomarkers

BiomarkerSourcePeakUse
GFAPAstrocytes24-48hFDA-approved (with UCH-L1) for mild TBI CT decision
UCH-L1Neurons6-12hNeuronal injury specificity
NfLAxons10-30dBest long-term DAI marker. Correlates with 6-12mo outcome.
S100BAstrocytes/Schwann6hRule-out in European guidelines (less specific)
TauAxons24-48hCorrelates with severity

Imaging

DAI CT — often normal
DAI MRI SWI — dark "blooming" foci
CT is often NORMAL in DAI. A normal CT in a comatose patient after significant trauma = suspect DAI. Only 10-20% show petechial hemorrhages on CT.

MRI Sensitivity by Sequence

  1. SWI (90-95%): Most sensitive. Detects 1-2mm hemosiderin foci. THE best sequence.
  2. GRE / T2* (70-80%): Hemorrhagic lesions via susceptibility artifact
  3. FLAIR (60-70%): Better for non-hemorrhagic DAI (hyperintense)
  4. DWI: Excellent acutely (restricted diffusion, first 48-72h)
  5. DTI: Research — quantitative tract disruption (FA reduction)

Lesion burden & outcomes: >15 lesions = poor. Brainstem = worst. Gray-white only = relatively better.

ICU Management: Prevent Secondary Insults

No surgical treatment for diffuse injury. The injured brain is extremely vulnerable to physiological derangements.

ParameterAvoidTargetHarm
HypotensionSBP <90SBP >100-110 (by age)Single episode DOUBLES mortality
HypoxiaPaO2 <60 / SpO2 <90PaO2 >100, SpO2 >95%Compounds ischemia
HyperthermiaTemp >38C36-37C+10% metabolic demand/degree
HyperglycemiaGlucose >180140-180Acidosis, edema (avoid <110 per NICE-SUGAR)
HyponatremiaNa <135140-155Worsens edema
AnemiaHgb <77-10Reduced O2 delivery
HypocapniaPaCO2 <35 (prolonged)35-40Vasoconstriction → ischemia
SeizuresAnyLEV prophylaxis; cEEG for GCS ≤8CMRO2 up 200-300%

Rehabilitation & Outcomes

Timeline

  • Acute (0-7d): ICU, prevent secondary injury
  • Subacute (1-4wk): Early rehab (PT/OT/SLP)
  • Recovery (1-12mo): Most rapid in first 3-6mo (neuroplasticity peak)
  • Chronic (>12mo): Slower, continued for years in younger patients
GradeIndependent LivingCommon Deficits
170-80%Attention, processing speed, executive function
240-60% moderate+Memory, executive (persistent)
320-30% moderate+Severe disability, vegetative common
Key prognostic indicators: Adams grade, age (younger better), coma duration (<2wk much better), MRI SWI lesion burden, NfL at day 10-14, SSEPs (bilateral absent N20 = very poor, but NOT 100% in trauma unlike cardiac arrest).

Quick-Reference Comparison

Feature EDH Acute SDH Chronic SDH tSAH Contusion DAI
SourceMMA (85%)Bridging veins (70%)NeomembranePial/cortical vesselsCortical microvesselsAxonal disruption
CT ShapeBiconvex (lens)CrescentCrescent (hypodense)Fills sulci"Salt & pepper"Often NORMAL
Crosses Sutures?NoYesYesN/AN/AN/A
Crosses Midline?Can (frontal/occipital)No (falx)No (falx)N/AN/AN/A
Lucid Interval?20-50%Rare (10-20%)N/AN/AN/ANo
Mortality5-12%50-90%0-5%Variable25-50%15-55%
Best ImagingCTCTMRI (isodense)CT / CTACT (serial)MRI (SWI)
Primary TxCraniotomyCraniotomy +/- DCBurr holes + drainSupportiveICP mgmt / surgeryPrevent 2° insults
Emergency Surgery?Yes (>15mm/shift)Yes (>10mm/shift)Elective/urgentRarelyIf criteria metNo surgical Tx

Herniation Syndromes

Uncal (Transtentorial) Herniation

Anatomy: Uncus/parahippocampal gyrus herniates over tentorial edge. Most common and clinically important.

1. EarlyIpsilateral pupil dilation (CN III parasympathetic first)
2. Early-midContralateral hemiparesis (peduncle)
3. MidDecreasing consciousness (RAS)
4. Mid-lateComplete CN III palsy (ptosis, "down & out")
5. LateBilateral fixed dilated, decerebrate
6. TerminalCushing triad, respiratory arrest

Also compresses: PCA (occipital infarct, 30-40%), contralateral peduncle (Kernohan notch, ipsilateral hemiparesis, 5%)

Intubate → Hyperventilate PaCO2 30-35 → Mannitol 1-1.5 g/kg or 23.4% NaCl 30 mL → HOB 30° → Emergent craniotomy

Subfalcine (Cingulate) Herniation

Anatomy: Cingulate gyrus herniates under falx cerebri.

  • Pericallosal artery (ACA): ACA infarction → contralateral leg weakness. Bilateral = abulia, akinetic mutism.
  • Foramen of Monro: Trapped ventricle → obstructive hydrocephalus → ICP spiral
  • Internal cerebral veins: Venous infarction
Often FIRST herniation to occur. Clinically subtle, detected on imaging (midline shift) before symptoms are prominent.

Central (Transtentorial) Herniation

Anatomy: Both hemispheres descend symmetrically through tentorial notch. Diffuse bilateral mass effect.

Plum & Posner Stages

StagePupilsMotorRespirations
DiencephalicSmall, reactive (1-3mm)DecorticateCheyne-Stokes
Upper midbrainMidposition, fixedDecerebrateCentral neurogenic hyperventilation
Lower midbrainMidposition, fixedFlaccidApneustic
MedullaryDilated, fixedFlaccidAtaxic → apnea
Duret hemorrhages (secondary brainstem hemorrhages from perforating vessel compression) are often fatal and NOT surgically treatable.

Tonsillar (Foramen Magnum) Herniation

Most rapidly fatal. Cerebellar tonsils through foramen magnum → medulla compression.

  • Occipital headache, neck stiffness (earliest)
  • Lower CN dysfunction (dysphagia, dysarthria)
  • Sudden respiratory arrest with minimal warning
  • Cardiovascular collapse, opisthotonus
LP is CONTRAINDICATED with posterior fossa mass. Emergency: intubate, osmotherapy, suboccipital craniectomy + C1 laminectomy.

Upward (Ascending) Transtentorial

Anatomy: Cerebellum herniates upward through tentorial notch.

  • Rapid coma, bilateral pupil dilation, upgaze palsy (Parinaud)
  • Acute obstructive hydrocephalus (blocked aqueduct)
  • SCA infarction, basilar perforator infarction
Iatrogenic risk: EVD placement in posterior fossa mass reduces supratentorial pressure → pulls cerebellum upward. Posterior fossa mass + hydrocephalus = decompression, NOT ventricular drainage alone.

Cushing Triad

Hypertension + Bradycardia + Irregular Respirations

  1. Rising ICP compresses RVLM (vasomotor center)
  2. Massive sympathetic discharge → hypertension (200-250 systolic)
  3. Baroreceptors → vagal response → bradycardia (HR 40-50)
  4. Respiratory center compression → irregular breathing
Present in only ~30% (low sensitivity). It is a LATE sign. If waiting for Cushing triad to diagnose herniation, you are waiting too long. The hypertension component alone may occur earlier.

GCS Calculator

Eye Opening (E: 1-4)

4Spontaneous — Eyes open at rest
3To voice — Opens to verbal stimulus
2To pressure — Opens to nail bed/trapezius
1None

Verbal Response (V: 1-5)

5Oriented — Name, place, date
4Confused — Conversational but disoriented
3Inappropriate words — Random single words
2Incomprehensible sounds — Moaning
1None

Motor Response (M: 1-6)

6Obeys commands — Two-step (not just grasp)
5Localizing — Crosses midline to stimulus
4Normal flexion — Withdrawal (spinal reflex)
3Abnormal flexion — Decorticate
2Extension — Decerebrate
1None

Pupil Reactivity (for GCS-P)

0Both reactive
1One reactive
2Neither reactive
--
Select scores above

Severity Classification

GCSSeverityDecision Points
13-15Mild TBICT if 13-14, or 15 with risk factors (anticoag, >65, LOC >5min, amnesia >30min, vomiting ≥2). Canadian CT Head Rule.
9-12Moderate TBIAll require CT. Admit. Consider ICP monitoring if abnormal CT + decline.
3-8Severe TBIIntubate. ICP monitoring if CT abnormal. Neurosurgery consult. ICU. Full TBI protocol.
Always record BEST response. Motor score is the single most prognostic component. Assess AFTER resuscitation. Document confounders: intubation (V=1T), periorbital swelling (E=1C), sedation, paralytics.

ICP Management

Monro-Kellie Doctrine

Skull = rigid box (~1,500 mL). Increase in one component must be compensated by decrease in another, or ICP rises.

ComponentVolume%
Brain parenchyma~1,200 mL80%
CSF~150 mL10%
Blood~150 mL10%

Compensation: CSF displacement (50-70 mL buffer) → venous compression (20-30 mL) → once exhausted: exponential ICP rise ("knee" of pressure-volume curve).

Normal ICP: 7-15 mmHg. Treatment threshold: >22 mmHg (BTF 2016). CPP target: 60-70 mmHg (CPP = MAP - ICP).

ICP Monitoring Indications (BTF 4th Ed)

  • GCS ≤8 + abnormal CT (any hemorrhage, edema, compressed cisterns, shift)
  • GCS ≤8 + normal CT if ≥2 of: age >40, posturing, SBP <90

EVD vs. Bolt

TypeAdvantagesDisadvantages
EVD (gold standard)Most accurate, recalibratable, can DRAIN CSF therapeuticallyHigher infection (5-10% ventriculitis), harder to place if compressed ventricles
Bolt (Codman, Camino)Easier placement, lower infection (1-3%), bedsideCannot drain CSF, cannot recalibrate, baseline drift over 5-7d

ICP Waveform

  • Normal: P1 > P2 > P3 (descending staircase)
  • Abnormal (P2 > P1): Decreased compliance. Brain on steep portion of curve. Treat aggressively even if mean ICP acceptable.
  • Lundberg A waves (plateau): ICP 50-100 mmHg x 5-20 min. Near-herniation events. EMERGENCY.
  • B waves: 0.5-2/min oscillations, 10-20 mmHg. Developing intracranial hypertension.

GENERAL General Measures (All Patients)

▼
  • HOB 30°, head midline (avoid jugular compression from neck rotation)
  • Loosen C-collar
  • Sedation: Propofol 20-75 mcg/kg/min or midazolam 0.02-0.1 mg/kg/h + fentanyl 1-2 mcg/kg/h
  • Temp: Normothermia 36-37C. Cooling for fever >38C (acetaminophen 1g q6h, blankets, intravascular cooling)
  • Seizure prophylaxis: LEV 500 mg BID. cEEG for GCS ≤8.
  • Glucose 140-180, Na 140-155, Hgb >7, SBP >100, SpO2 >95%

TIER 1 First-Line Interventions

▼

1. CSF Drainage via EVD

  • Open to drain at 10-15 cmH2O (~7-11 mmHg)
  • Removes ~5-10 mL per episode. Immediate ICP reduction.
  • Caution: over-drainage → slit ventricle, upward herniation, subdural hygroma

2. Optimize Sedation

  • Propofol up to 75-80 mcg/kg/min (propofol infusion syndrome: acidosis, rhabdo, hyperK — limit <48h at high doses)
  • Bolus: propofol 1-2 mg/kg or midazolam 2-5 mg IV

3. Mild Hyperventilation

  • Target PaCO2 35-38 (from normal 40)
  • CO2 is vasodilator; lowering it → vasoconstriction → decreased CBV → decreased ICP
  • Onset 30 seconds. Effect temporary (hours, tachyphylaxis). Avoid <30 (ischemia).

TIER 2 Second-Line (if ICP >22 despite Tier 1)

▼

1. Osmotherapy

Mannitol 20%Hypertonic Saline
Dose0.25-1 g/kg IV bolus q4-6h23.4%: 30 mL central line only
3%: 150-250 mL bolus or 30-100 mL/h
MechanismOsmotic gradient + rheological vasoconstrictionOsmotic gradient + volume expansion
MonitoringOsm q6h. Hold if >320 (ATN risk)Na q4-6h. Target 145-155. Max 160.
Onset15-30 min; 4-6h duration23.4%: 5-10 min. 3%: gradual.
AdvantageCheaper, peripheral line OKNo renal toxicity, volume-expanding
COBI (2024): No difference between mannitol and HTS in 6-month outcomes. Both acceptable. Mannitol if normovolemic; HTS if hypotensive.

2. Neuromuscular Blockade

  • Cisatracurium 0.15 mg/kg bolus then 1-3 mcg/kg/min
  • Eliminates coughing/straining. Must sedate BEFORE paralysis. cEEG required (masks seizures).

TIER 3 Salvage (Refractory ICP)

▼

1. Barbiturate Coma

  • Pentobarbital: Load 10 mg/kg over 30 min, then 5 mg/kg/h x 3, then 1-3 mg/kg/h
  • Reduces CMRO2 by 50% → coupling-mediated vasoconstriction → decreased ICP
  • Titrate to burst suppression on cEEG
  • Major: hypotension (need vasopressors), immunosuppression, hepatotoxicity, loss of neuro exam for days

2. Therapeutic Hypothermia (Controversial)

  • Target 33-35C. CMRO2 decreases ~7%/degree below 37C.
  • Eurotherm3235 (2015): WORSE outcomes as primary ICP Rx. Limited, controversial use.

3. Decompressive Craniectomy

  • Last resort per RESCUEicp. Large flap ≥12x15 cm removed, dura opened.
  • Morbidity: hydrocephalus (20-30%), wound complications, syndrome of trephined

Key Dosing Quick Reference

MedicationIndicationDoseMonitoring
LevetiracetamSeizure prophylaxis500 mg IV/PO BID x 7dRenal dosing CrCl <50
Mannitol 20%ICP reduction0.25-1 g/kg IV q4-6hOsm q6h, hold >320
23.4% NaClAcute herniation30 mL IV (central line)Na q4h, target 145-155
3% NaClICP infusion150-250 mL bolus or 30-100 mL/hNa q4h, target 145-155
PentobarbitalBarbiturate comaLoad 10 mg/kg, then 1-3 mg/kg/hcEEG, BP
4F-PCCWarfarin reversal25-50 u/kg by INRINR 30min, 6h, 24h
IdarucizumabDabigatran reversal5 g IV (two 2.5g)Thrombin time
PropofolICU sedation20-80 mcg/kg/minTG, CK, lactate
NimodipineVasospasm prevention60 mg PO/NG q4h x 21dBP (hypotension)
